projects.title: EtherLocked

content.text: EtherLocked is a first-person puzzle game that I developed together with two classmates and my brother over the course of a year as a school project. Working in a small team of three gave us the chance to explore every stage of development closely, from concept to final build, while each of us took on multiple roles.

I did the 2D aspects of the game, shaping its visual identity and player experience. I designed the main menu and user interface, ensuring a clear and immersive flow for players. I made the level design and environment design, combining functionality with atmosphere to guide exploration. In addition, I created concept art to support the team’s vision, helping establish the game’s mood and aesthetic direction.

I contributed to the 3D aspects of the game, creating and implementing various objects used throughout the environment. I was responsible for modeling assets, assigning and adjusting pre-existing materials, and applying stickers and textures. I imported and arranged the models in Unity.
More in-game pictures, sketches, out-of-bounds pictures, layouts etc.

projects.title: Suburbs

content.text: Drive a tiny matchbox car down an infinite road, blasting through waves of giant enemies like rats and raccoons while avoiding cans that can destroy your vehicle.

Suburbs is a top-down shoot 'em up video game that I developed alone over the course of a half a year as a school project. Working independently gave me the opportunity to experience and overcome every development stage, error and to learn the fundamentals of game design first-hand.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Court Cheester

content.text: Señor Prankster Pipsqueak, a brave little mouse, goes to the castle to prove that mice aren’t frightening but full of joy. His mission: make the king laugh and change how the world sees his kind.

Court Cheester is a arcade hopper game that I developed together with two classmates over the course of weekend as a game jam project. This was my first ever game project finished in a game jam. With only a few days, a tiny team, we explored every stage of development to make a playable experience.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: The Cuise

content.text: A deep-sea mission aboard a submarine turns into a desperate fight for survival when a monstrous, venomous creatures emerges from the darkness.

The Cuise is a roguelike shoot 'em up video game that I developed together with three classmates over the course of a weekend as a game jam project. With only a few days, a tiny team, and long nights of work, we pushed through the entire development cycle to make a complete playable experience.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Owl

content.text: I created a small stylized owl asset, following a game-ready pipeline from sculpting to engine testing. I sculpted the owl in Zbrush, then textured it in Substance Painter. After completing the model, I prepared it for real-time use and imported it into Unity, where I tested it to ensure it behaved correctly and was fully optimized for gameplay. I made a scene in Blender to showcase it with the drawer asset I made for our game, EtherLocked.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Pipes and wrench

content.text: This scene combines two assets I originally created in separate school classes: a wrench and a set of industrial pipes. A year later, I revisited both pieces, corrected and optimized the original meshes, then created procedural textures for the pipes and wrench to give them a consistent, realistic material surface. To enhance the presentation, I tested multiple HDRI lighting setups in Blender, choosing those that best showcased the metal textures, reflections, and wear details.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Mimic Chest

content.text: For this project, I created a mimic, exploring the complete 3D workflow from sculpting to animation. I began by sculpting the creature’s form and details in ZBrush. Afterward, I textured the model in Substance Painter. Once the model was finalized, I imported it into Blender, where I created the rig and set up controls for movement. I then produced a short animation to bring the mimic to life, testing how the sculpt, textures, and rig worked together in motion.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Diorama

content.text: I created this low-poly train station diorama in Blender, featuring flat shading and procedurally generated vegetation using geometry nodes. For this project I focused on scene polishing and experimenting with shader nodes and settings to create a more lively and cohesive environment. For me in every project I make having an easily understandable order system throughout my files is very important. I used PureRef program for reference images so I have a clear goal.

projects.footerCopy: © All Rights Reserved By Forgach Rebeka

projects.title: Park environment

content.text: I wanted to create multiple game ready objects in a short period of time (approximately one week) for a top-down game idea. Because of the speed and the genre of the game I went with low-poly models.

I modeled everything keeping in mind the low poly count and where it was due I shaded the edges smooth (or in the case of the benches, I mostly used weighted normals). I made sure to delete the faces that are not going to be visible.
highlights: delete the faces that are not going to be visible

content.text: At the beginning I was going to make textures in Substance Painter, so I made a high poly sandwich where the different ingredients could be separated as my first model.
Later on there was no need for these assets so I simplified them as much as I could.

After making sure that all the assets have their origin point at a reasonable place and all the transformations are applied, I knew there can’t be a lot of surprises when I import them into Unity.

After importing every FBX file into Unity, I made a “zoo” scene, where all the prefabs are showcased in a dedicated scene so every color and size can be seen immediately, and find assets more easily. I created materials for them inside the engine.
highlights: origin point at a reasonable place
created materials for them inside the engine

content.text: I also created an example environment out of these assets.

projects.title: False Sun

content.text: False Sun is a twin-stick shooter with tower defense elements. This game is still in development together with five of my classmates over the course of a half a year as a school project. In this project I am the lead environment artist and we explored every stage of development closely, from concept to final build, while each of us took on multiple roles.
I did all the props for the game after researching and collecting reference images. I made the level design and environment design, combining functionality with atmosphere to guide exploration.
Retopology

I also had to retopologize an asset I got from the team. There were n-gons, not needed details and faces that were not connected to anything. I cleaned up all unnecessary faces and made sure unity can triangulate faces in a logical way without any n-gons.

I was responsible to create the modular models for the houses. I created and implemented various wall types, roofs etc. used throughout the environment. Furthermore, I built all the houses used in the level.

I imported and arranged the models in Unity, inside a scene called the “zoo” where every prefab can be viewed in the scene with all the colors and sizes.

The team chose a cartoonish style for the game and to achieve it I created two custom shaders in Unity.

The first shader was a basic toon shader creating stepped, cartoon like shading effect. With this approach areas facing away from the light became completely black. To fix this, I developed a second shader that introduces a minimum light threshold so even the darkest areas retain some visible color.
